senses.gloss: except (for), save (for), but (for), barring; other than, besides, apart from

examples.english: I could see them all except you.

examples.text: لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَا ءَالِهَةٌ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتَا فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ
examples.english: Had there been within them [i.e., the heavens and earth] any gods besides Allāh, they both would have been ruined. So exalted is Allāh, the Lord of the Throne, above what they describe.

senses.gloss: no more than; nothing more than; nothing but or no one but; only, but

examples.text: الصِّحَّةُ تَاجٌ عَلَى رُؤُوسِ الْأَصِحَّاءِ لَا يَرَاهُ إِلَّا الْمَرْضَى.
examples.english: Health is a crown that is on the heads of the healthy, which only the ill can see.

examples.text: إِنْ هُوَ إِلاَّ ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَالَمِينَ 27 لِمَن شَاء مِنكُمْ أَن يَسْتَقِيمَ 28 وَمَا تَشَاؤُونَ إِلاَّ أَن يَشَاء اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ 29
examples.english: 27 This is but a reminder to the worlds, 28 to those of you who wish to straighten out themselves, 29 and you never wish it except when God wishes it, the Lord of the Worlds.

senses.gloss: except (for), save (for), but (for), barring; other than, besides, apart from

examples.text:
مولاي إني ببابك قد بسطت يدي
من لي ألوذ به إلاك يا سندي
examples.english:
My Lord, I sit at your door, with arms outspread.
Who is there for me to whom I may flee,
Save for you, my prop?

senses.gloss: no more than; nothing more than; nothing but or no one but; only, but

etymology.text: Cognate with Ge'ez አላ (ʾälla), Hebrew אֶלָּא (ʾellā).

, (Mughnee at the end of the article on this word,) is used in four manners. (The same in the beginning of the art.) First, (Mughnee,) it is used (as a particle, S, Msb,) to denote exception; [meaning
as will be seen below;] (T, S, Msb, Mughnee, K; [in which last it is mentioned in art.

when the sentence is negative, and

is a conjunction, like the conjunctive

is here in the nom. case because the sentence commences with a negative: (T:) or in a sentence [like this,] which is not affirmative, in which the thing excepted is united in kind to that from which the exception is made, accord. to the opinion which is generally preferred and which commonly obtains, the noun signifying the thing excepted is a substitute for the noun signifying that from which the exception is made; but it is allowable to put it in the accus. case according to the general rule respecting exception; so that one says,
There stood not any one, except Zeyd
]: and the same is the case in a prohibitive sentence; as in
Let not any one stand, except Zeyd
]; and in an interrogative sentence; as in
Did any one stand, except Zeyd?
]; when, in such sentences, the thing excepted is united in kind to that from which the exception is made. (I 'Ak p. 162.) You say also,
], without mentioning that from which the exception is made; (TA;) and
]; (I' Ak p. 164;) the case of the noun signifying the thing excepted being the same as if

, or the like. (I 'Ak ubi suprà.) When the thing excepted precedes that from which the exception is made, if the sentence is affirmative, the noun signifying the former must be in the accus. case; as in
]: and so, accord. to the usage generally preferred, when the sentence is not affirmative; as in
]; but recorded instances allow one's saying also,

. (I 'Ak p. 163.) When the thing excepted is disunited in kind from that from which the exception is made, if the sentence is affirmative, the noun signifying the former must likewise be in the accus. case; as in
I beat the people, but not an ass
], &c.: (I' Ak p. 162:) and so, accord. to the generality of the Arabs, when the sentence is negative; as in

I saw not the people, but an ass

accord. to the more approved usage],

body.text: That there may not be to men, against you, any allegation, and

]; (Msb, Mughnee, K;) occurring in the Kur [ii. 145]; (Msb;) so accord. to Akh and Fr and AO; (Mughnee;) i. e., and those who have acted wrongfully also, to them there shall not be, against you, any allegation: (Msb:) Fr explains it as meaning that the wrongdoer has no allegation of which account should be taken; and this is correct, and is the opinion held by Zj. (T.) Thus, too, in the saying [in the Kur xxvii. 10 and 11],

body.text_bw: laA yaxaAfu ladaY~a A@lomurosaluwna A_il~aA mano Zalama
body.text_ar: لَا يَخَافُ لَدَىَّ ا@لْمُرْسَلُونَ اـِلَّا مَنْ ظَلَمَ

body.text: The apostles shall not fear in my presence, and
he who hath acted wrongfully, then hath done good instead, after evil;
as some explain it; but others say that
here denotes exception]. (Mughnee, in which it is explained as meaning ; and K.) And thus in the saying of the poet, [namely, El-Mukhabbal Es-Saadee, (S in art.

waA^araY lahaA daArFA biA^agodirap Also
                      
                      
                        siydaAni lamo yadoruso lahaA rasomu
                      
                      
                        A_il~aA ramaAdFA haAmidFA dafaEato
                      
                      
                        Eanohu Alr~iyaAHa xawaAlidN suHomN
belonging to her, at the pools of Es-Seedán,
the remains of which have not become effaced, and ashes wasted and compacted together, from which three black pieces of stone whereon the cooking-pot was wont to be placed turned back the winds
. (S.) ―         -b4-  Fourthly, (Mughnee,) it is redundant, as in the following verse, (S in art.
, Mughnee, K,) of Dhu-rRummeh, (S ubi suprà, Mughnee,) accord. to As and IJ: (Mughnee:)

HaraAjiyju maA tanofak~u A_il~aA munaAxapN
                      
                      
                        EalaY Alxasofi A^awo naromiY bihaA baladFA qaforaA
(but other meanings are assigned to the word which I thus render,)
that cease not to be made to lie down in a state of hunger, or with which we direct our course to a desert region
]; (S ubi suprà, Mughnee; [but in one copy of the former, in the place of
; and in my copy of the latter,
: (S ubi suprà:) but it is said that this is a mistake of the poet: (Mughnee:) so says Aboo-'Amr Ibn-El-'Alà; for, he says,
is not to be introduced after
: (TA:) and some say that the right reading is
[in a pl. sense]: and some, that
is a complete [or an attributive] verb, and
is a denotative of state; [consequently, that
, as in some other instances hereafter to be mentioned;] the meaning being,
not free, from fatigue [unless when made to lie down
]. (Mughnee.) The following is also given as an ex. of the same kind:
]: but the reading which is remembered to have been heard is
: and if the former be correct, it may be explained on the supposition that
is the complement of an oath meant to be understood, and that
is suppressed, as in [the saying in the Kur xii. 85,]
save a water-wheel, with its people;
] the form of the exceptive sentence which is devoid of the mention of that from which the exception is made indicating such an explanation. (Mughnee.) ―         -b5-  [Fifthly,] it occurs as
[as a particle denoting exception, equivalent to our
and (after an oath or the like)
]; as in the saying in the Kur [xxxviii. 13],
There was not any one but
], in which 'Abd-Allah reads, in its place,
beseech, thee by God but that thou give me;
i. e., I do not ask of thee anything save thy giving me; the preterite here, as in many instances in which it is preceded by
(q. v.), not being a preterite in meaning]; for which one says also
. (T.)       -A2-  It is also a particle [or rather a compound of two words] denoting the complement of a condition; originally
, which form a compound that does not admit of [the pronunciation termed] imáleh, because
] It is followed by a fut., which it renders mejzoom; [and in this case it may be rendered as above, or by
] as in the saying in the Kur [viii. 74],
unless ye do it, there will be a weakness of faith and an appearing of unbelief in the earth
]. (T.) [In like manner,] in a saying such as the following, [in the Kur ix. 40,]
will not, aid him, certainly God aided him
], it is only a compound of two words, the conditional

of which the usages have been mentioned before, though Ibn-Málik has included it therewith. (Mughnee.) [Often in post-classical works, and perhaps in classical also, but seldom except when it is preceded by a condition with its complement, the verb or verbal proposition which should immediately follow it is suppressed; as in the like of the saying,
If thou do such a thing, I forgive thee,

sometimes also it ends a sentence, by an aposiopesis; the whole of what should follow it being suppressed: and sometimes the complement of the condition which precedes, as well as the verb or verbal proposition which should immediately follow it, is suppressed; so that you say,
If thou do such a thing,
excellent will it be, or the like,
but if not, I kill thee.
Hence,] it sometimes has the meaning of
denoting an alternative, corresponding to a preceding
, which signifies “either,”] as in the saying,
Either do thou speak to me or else
or if thou wilt not speak to me
in the Kur vi. 111, &c. And by
as a denotative of state, as in
Do not ye die unless ye be Muslims;
in the Kur ii. 126 and iii. 97. And sometimes it is preceded by
; for the effect of which, in this case, see art.
